name: LIQUID PAGE - shader-dissolve multi-page transitions, each page a full distinct scene
ref: Codrops 'Building a Scroll-Revealed WebGL Gallery with Three.js and Barba.js' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops); multi-page WebGL transition portfolio pattern in Codrops Creative Hub 2026
palette: #070708 (void) + scene-specific accent per page: page 1 amber #e8b84b, page 2 cyan #00d4ff, page 3 coral #ff6b6b; transitions are the palette shift
brief: A multi-page SPA (index + 3 work detail pages) where navigating between pages triggers a full-viewport WebGL shader transition: a noise-masked dissolve sweeping diagonally that reveals the next page's color accent. Each page is a distinct full-scene composition (index is a minimal type list; each detail is a full-bleed cinematic spread) so the transition IS the experience of moving between visual worlds. Distinct from view-transition-morph (single shared-element morphs between index card and detail; same color world; no WebGL) and fold-origami (within-page CSS 3D fold; single page). One detail page link is pre-hovered at scroll=0. WebGL: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use a LUT uniform for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:false; canvas paints before render loop starts. Mobile: WebGL transition replaced by CSS clip-path diagonal wipe. Guardrail: one Three.js canvas persists across routes, only geometry/texture swaps; DPR capped at 1; routing via native History API pushState. Three.js via importmap; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: GOMMAGE - WebGPU TSL shader dissolves MSDF headline type into dust and petals on scroll
ref: Codrops 'WebGPU Gommage Effect: Dissolving MSDF Text into Dust and Petals with Three.js and TSL' (January 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/01/28); WebGPU Three.js TSL workflow (Codrops WebGPU tag)
brief: Each work section's headline is rendered as MSDF text in a Three.js WebGPU scene; scroll progress drives a noise-threshold TSL shader that disintegrates the letterforms into amber petals and ice-dust particles, revealing the work asset beneath. The dissolve reads as a dramatic title card that self-destructs to show the piece. Distinct from dithered-lo-fi (WebGL Bayer-matrix dither on images, not text dissolve), fluid-xray-reveal (cursor-driven FBO fluid mask on images), and tech-organic-mesh (GLSL sine-displacement fragment shader full-viewport background). WebGPU: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use a LUT texture for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:true; canvas sized to hero viewport only. Selective bloom via MRT. Mobile: WebGL fallback with a CSS clip-path wipe on the headline (no particle burst). Guardrail: particle count capped at 8,000; TSL compute dispatched only when hero is in view; DPR capped at 1 on mobile. Three.js via importmap; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: BURST IN - @starting-style discrete-entry animation drives every card's entrance with zero JS
ref: @starting-style CSS rule (Baseline 2025, MDN; Bramus Van Damme 'Unleash the Power of @starting-style' CSS Day 2025); CSS @starting-style gallery entrances (Codrops 2025-2026 CSS primitives roundup, tympanus.net/codrops)
palette: #f9f6f0 (archival paper) + #111 (ink) + #c94a2e (vermilion burst ring); media supplies all other color
brief: A grid of work cards that enter the DOM one at a time (staggered via JS classList.add) but ALL transition logic lives in CSS @starting-style: each card has a declared start state (scale 0.6, opacity 0, blur 8px, rotate 8deg) inside @starting-style, and the settled target state is the normal rule, so the browser interpolates from the starting style on first paint without any JS-driven keyframes. Clicking a tag filter removes and re-inserts cards, triggering fresh @starting-style bursts. Distinct from scroll-snap-choreography (JS IntersectionObserver toggles animation-play-state on sections; @keyframes, not @starting-style), typewriter-reveal-grid (IntersectionObserver triggers per-char typing; not using @starting-style discrete entry), and zero-js-scroll-timeline (CSS scroll-driven animations with view() on pre-existing DOM; no DOM insertion). The initial grid load fires all bursts in a 50ms staggered cascade at scroll=0 so the mechanic is unmissable. @supports (selector(:has(+ *))) gate (same support matrix as @starting-style); fallback is instant grid. No canvas; no WebGL. All SVG fill/stroke hex, no CSS var() in presentation attributes. Mobile: same mechanic; grid narrows to 2 columns. Guardrail: stagger delay capped at 600ms total; reduced-motion collapses all @starting-style to opacity:1 only. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: VORONOI FIELD - works mapped to Voronoi cells on Canvas 2D; click a cell to expand and lock it
ref: Godly 2026 spatial/generative gallery category (godly.design); CSS Design Awards generative-geometry interactive 2026 (cssdesignawards.com); d3-delaunay Voronoi techniques (Observable Plot 2026 generative-layout examples)
palette: #0c0c10 (void) + #f0ece4 (bone cell borders + labels) + #c94a2e (vermilion active seed ring) + #e8c547 (amber expanded cell fill)
brief: Work seed points are distributed spatially on a Canvas 2D viewport using Fortune's sweep-line algorithm (implemented from scratch, no d3 or external lib). Each Voronoi cell clips its work thumbnail to the cell polygon via Canvas 2D clip(). Hovering the canvas finds the nearest seed point (distance comparison, no quadtree needed at under 12 works) and highlights that cell with a bone border and a pulsing vermilion seed ring. Clicking a cell expands it by pushing all neighboring seed points outward (spring animation on seed positions, cells recompute each frame), revealing the full work in the enlarged region; a second click collapses. Distinct from particle-constellation (static star-map of work nodes with constellation lines; no Voronoi tessellation; no clip geometry), infinite-canvas (pan/zoom spatial board with CSS transform; works are rectangular cards, not tessellated cells), and ripple-click-canvas (expanding wavefront ring unlocks blurred grid cards; no Voronoi geometry). The initial tessellation with all cells naturally filled is the full scroll=0 state. Canvas 2D; no WebGL. Mobile: tap to expand; pinch ignored (canvas fills viewport). Guardrail: canvas resizes on ResizeObserver and recomputes Voronoi from scratch (fast at under 12 seeds); reduced-motion disables spring animation (instant cell snap). All seed-label SVG fill/stroke in hex in any inline annotation layer. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: GRAY-SCOTT - a live Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion system fills the canvas; works grow from seeds in the pattern
ref: Codrops 'Reaction-Diffusion Simulation on Canvas' creative coding archive (tympanus.net/codrops/tag/canvas/); Karl Sims Gray-Scott model (canonical reference, karlsims.com/rd.html); reaction-diffusion generative portfolios (Creative Applications Network 2025-2026, creativeapplications.net)
palette: #030508 (abyss) + chemical A #e8f5ec (ice-green) + chemical B #1a6b4a (deep teal); pattern IS the color system; work card accent = amber #e8c547
brief: A full-viewport Canvas 2D Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion simulation runs as a living background: two-chemical discretized PDE on a 400x400 grid (bilinear upscaled to canvas), iterated 8 steps per rAF frame, rendered with a lookup color table mapping concentration B to the palette (not a dynamic-indexed GLSL array; this is CPU JS, no WebGL). Work thumbnails are seeded into the system as circular low-B seeds; the reaction-diffusion pattern organically grows outward and wraps around each work, making the generative biology frame each piece. Hovering a work suppresses the reaction near it (pausing the seed), click locks it and pauses diffusion there. Distinct from tech-organic-mesh (GLSL sine-wave fragment shader; GPU-side WebGL, not PDE simulation; no biological pattern-formation), noise-displacement-hover (Perlin noise field card displacement; cursor push interaction; not a reaction-diffusion chemical system), and offscreen-canvas-substrate (value-noise color field on OffscreenCanvas worker; uniform noise topology, not two-chemical PDE). The pattern is already mid-simulation with visible spots or stripes at scroll=0. Pure Canvas 2D on main thread (400x400 grid is fast enough); no WebGL. Mobile: grid halved to 200x200. Guardrail: rAF pauses when tab hidden (document.hidden); reduced-motion pauses simulation after first 120 frames (freeze on a visually rich frame). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PATH MORPH - clip-path:path() morphs between two authored SVG shapes on scroll, cropping each work
ref: CSS clip-path:path() (Baseline 2025, MDN; Codrops 'Animating clip-path: path()' 2025 tympanus.net/codrops); scroll-driven clip-path path-morph (Smashing Magazine CSS advanced clip-path 2026)
palette: #f5f1eb (warm bone) + #111 (ink) + #2c6fad (cobalt morph seam); per-work image supplies color
brief: Each work panel has a clip-path that morphs between two authored path() shapes as it scrolls through the viewport: the entering shape is a jagged lightning-bolt polygon (all 16 control points authored once in a JS array) and the resting shape is a clean rectangle. The morph is purely CSS: animation-timeline:view() scrubs a @keyframes from the authored path string to 'path(rect equivalent)' using the same node count. At scroll=0 the first panel sits frozen mid-morph showing the jagged clip eating into the image, making the mechanic immediately readable. Distinct from blind-mask-wipe (SVG <rect> elements retract row-by-row; not clip-path:path(); not a shape-morph), fold-origami (CSS 3D rotateY on a 3D hinge; not clip-path), and zero-js-scroll-timeline (per-element entrance variety with iris, blur, rotate; none use clip-path:path() shape morphing). @supports (clip-path: path('M0 0')) gate; fallback is clip-path:inset(). No canvas; no WebGL. All inline SVG fill/stroke hex not CSS var() in presentation attributes. Mobile: same mechanic, viewport-relative path coordinates. Guardrail: path node count fixed at 16 per shape (same count required for path interpolation); reduced-motion collapses animation to opacity fade. Native CSS only for animation; one small JS block maps scroll position only for older browsers without view() support. NO GSAP or external libs.

name: OKLCH MIX - a single base hue drives every accent via color-mix() in oklch; a hue slider shifts the entire palette live
ref: CSS color-mix() in oklch (Baseline 2023; Smashing Magazine 'Wide-Gamut Colors with CSS' 2025; Adam Argyle 'oklch color pickers' 2026 web.dev); CSS @property + color-mix() live theming (CSS-Tricks 2026, css-tricks.com/color-mix)
palette: Single @property --hue (0 to 360); card 1 = color-mix(in oklch, oklch(70% 0.2 var(--hue)), white 30%); card 2 = color-mix(in oklch, oklch(50% 0.25 calc(var(--hue) + 60deg)), black 20%); neutral background = color-mix(in oklch, oklch(15% 0.02 var(--hue)), black 80%)
brief: The entire gallery palette is computed from ONE CSS custom property --hue (an @property <number>) via color-mix() in oklch: each card's accent, the background, the type tint, and the border glow are all color-mix() expressions referencing --hue with different lightness and chroma offsets. A sticky hue-wheel control (a round <input type=range> styled with CSS accent-color and rotated via CSS) animates --hue in real time, making the entire page shift hue simultaneously with perceptually uniform steps (oklch keeps lightness constant). On scroll each card enters with a ::before sweep that uses color-mix() against the card's own accent. Distinct from arc (chromatic scroll-story where page color temperature shifts cold to warm to hot across fixed palette bands; no color-mix() or @property hue; static per-section colors), retro-futurism-holo (conic-gradient foil with @property hue-rotate on a fixed decoration layer; not a whole-page dynamic theming system), and time-of-day (five hardcoded palette bands keyed to real clock time; no user-driven hue). The gallery is already rendered in mid-hue at scroll=0 with the hue-wheel visible and labeled. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: hue wheel collapses to a bottom-fixed bar input. Guardrail: @property --hue registered as <number> syntax with inherits:true; color-mix() fallback is a static oklch() value; reduced-motion disables the per-card sweep (instant reveal). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: COMPOSITE ROOM - overlapping work layers each with a distinct CSS mix-blend-mode; scroll moves layers apart or together
ref: CSS mix-blend-mode creative stacking (Codrops 'Blend Modes & Compositing' 2025-2026, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/blend-mode/); CSS Design Awards blend-mode portfolio exploration 2026 (cssdesignawards.com)
palette: #f0ece4 (bone base) + primary-color layers: work 1 multiply #c94a2e (vermilion), work 2 screen #2c6fad (cobalt), work 3 overlay #e8c547 (amber), work 4 difference #3ab8a8 (teal); compositing IS the color system
brief: Four work layers are stacked in exact 2D registration (all positioned at the same coordinates, each a full-viewport element) with each layer assigned a distinct CSS mix-blend-mode: multiply, screen, overlay, difference. At scroll=0 all four layers fully overlap and the additive composite of all four images is the hero visual. Scroll drives translateX offsets on each layer at different rates (not parallax depth; lateral separation): by mid-scroll the layers have slid apart to reveal individual works; fully scrolled they are in a non-overlapping quad layout. The compositeing moment in between is the signature. Distinct from split-diptych (one cursor-dragged seam between two clip-path halves; binary not blended), parallax-depth (multi-layer Z-depth diorama with translateZ; no blend modes), and fluid-xray-reveal (WebGL FBO fluid mask dissolves outer to inner layer; single card at a time; no blend-mode stacking). The fully overlapping composite with all four blend modes active is the immediate scroll=0 state. No canvas; no WebGL; pure CSS transform + mix-blend-mode. All inline SVG fill/stroke hex. Mobile: vertical separation instead of horizontal; same blend modes. Guardrail: isolation:isolate on the wrapper confines compositing to the group; reduced-motion disables lateral animation (static quad layout). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: GYRO GALLERY - works arranged in a hemisphere; continuous DeviceOrientation tilt pans the view like a gyroscope viewfinder
ref: DeviceOrientationEvent API (MDN; iOS gyroscope + JS portfolio explorations, Awwwards mobile-first interaction 2026, awwwards.com); immersive tilt-to-pan spatial portfolio (Godly mobile-first 2026, godly.design)
palette: #08080c (abyss) + #f2ede4 (bone card face) + #c94a2e (vermilion active reticle) + #e8c547 (amber horizon ring)
brief: Up to 9 works are placed at fixed angular positions on a virtual hemisphere (precalculated spherical coordinates: azimuth 0-315deg in 45deg steps, elevation 0 or +20deg). The CSS perspective viewport maps these angular positions to 2D via simple orthographic projection updated each DeviceOrientation event (alpha/beta/gamma from the real gyroscope). Tilting the phone left/right/up/down pans the virtual camera across the hemisphere and cards drift to the new viewport projection. A vermilion reticle stays screen-center; the card that enters the reticle for 1 second auto-locks and expands. On desktop (no gyroscope) the cursor position maps to the same alpha/beta/gamma range. Distinct from lenticular-flip (device tilt triggers a binary face-flip, not continuous 2-axis spatial pan of many cards), depth-map-tilt (per-pixel depth bands on a single card; not a multi-card spatial arrangement on a hemisphere), and parallax-depth (multi-layer Z-depth diorama driven by cursor; not a spherical coordinate gallery). At scroll=0 (or page load) the hemisphere is visible from the default camera bearing, with 3 to 4 cards partially in frame and the reticle showing. Requests DeviceMotionEvent permission on iOS; graceful fallback to cursor-only on permission deny. Mobile primary; desktop cursor fallback. Guardrail: alpha/beta/gamma smoothed via exponential filter (factor 0.15) before projection; reduced-motion disables all camera movement (static front-facing grid). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PAINT FORGE - CSS Houdini registerPaint() worklets draw live geometric backgrounds on each card, animated via @property
ref: CSS Houdini Paint API / CSS.paintWorklet (MDN; 'CSS Houdini Is Ready' Smashing Magazine 2025; Houdini.how worklet gallery 2025-2026, houdini.how); @property + CSS Paint Worklet animated patterns (web.dev/css-houdini 2025)
palette: #1a1a1e (graphite card body) + CSS-painted geometry per card: card 1 circles #c94a2e, card 2 sine wave #2c6fad, card 3 voronoi seed lines #e8c547, card 4 diagonal hash #3ab8a8; @property drives all geometry via --paint-progress
brief: Each work card's background-image is a CSS paint() function call referencing a registered Houdini PaintWorklet: card 1 = 'checkerboard' worklet (concentric animated circles), card 2 = 'sinewave' worklet, card 3 = 'seedlines' worklet, card 4 = 'hatch' worklet. A shared @property --paint-progress (type <number>, range 0..1) is animated via hover transition and drives geometry parameters inside each worklet's paint() function (line density, radius, amplitude). Hovering a card injects energy into its --paint-progress, morphing the live-drawn geometry. Scroll=0 shows all cards with their default paint states already rendering distinct geometric backgrounds behind their work thumbnails. @supports (background: paint(id)) gate; fallback is a static CSS gradient per card. Worklet code is a single inline Blob URL (no external .js file fetch). No canvas element; no WebGL; worklets draw directly into each card's background layer. Mobile: @property hover replaced by touch-start; worklets are same. Guardrail: worklets register once via CSS.paintWorklet.addModule() gated behind @supports; each card's geometry is self-contained with no shared mutable state; reduced-motion freezes --paint-progress at 0.3. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: STROKE FIRST - SVG paint-order:stroke fill + vector-effect:non-scaling-stroke makes giant hairline-outlined art-director type that never fattens on scale
ref: SVG paint-order:stroke fill (MDN; CSS Design Awards typographic poster 2026, cssdesignawards.com poster-series category); SVG vector-effect:non-scaling-stroke art-director type (Codrops SVG advanced techniques roundup 2025, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/svg/)
palette: #f4f0e6 (bone stock) + #111 (ink fill) + hairline stroke #c94a2e (vermilion at 1.5px non-scaling); work images sit behind the type layer; per-poster accent overlay
brief: Each scroll panel is a full-viewport inline SVG poster where display headlines are drawn as SVG <text> elements with paint-order:stroke fill and a 6px stroke that paint-order renders BEHIND the fill (so the stroke expands outward only, never eating the counter spaces). vector-effect:non-scaling-stroke on each <text> means the 1.5px outline stays pixel-crisp regardless of viewBox scale or zoom; no stroke thickening at large sizes. Scroll drives a scale() transform on the SVG viewBox that zooms into each poster's focal word while the hairline maintains its hairline weight. Type size is at minimum 24vw at rest. Distinct from blueprint-svg (SVG stroke-dashoffset line-art drawing itself, engineering blueprint; not display type with paint-order/vector-effect), text-on-path-poster (SVG textPath curves with stroke-dashoffset draw-on; curves are the mechanic, not paint-order stroke rendering), and swiss-poster (CSS HTML grid poster; no inline SVG text with paint-order). The first poster is fully composed with the giant hairline-outlined headline visible at scroll=0. All SVG fill/stroke are hex literals in presentation attributes, never CSS var(). Mobile: SVG viewBox rescales; vector-effect ensures stroke stays hairline. Guardrail: SVG text is user-selectable (no convert-to-path); reduced-motion disables the viewBox zoom (static poster stack). Native CSS/JS/SVG only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: HIGHLIGHT WAVE - CSS Custom Highlight API marks matching keywords across all work descriptions simultaneously as a wavefront rolls down the page
ref: CSS Custom Highlight API (Baseline 2024, MDN; Codrops 'Highlighting Text Ranges with the Custom Highlight API' 2025, tympanus.net/codrops); CSS ::highlight() selector + Highlight registry (web.dev/custom-highlight-api 2025)
palette: #faf7f2 (warm paper) + #111 (ink) + #e8c547 (amber highlight) + #c94a2e (vermilion active-keyword ring); Newsreader + Space Mono
brief: A typographic work index shows each work as a prose description paragraph. On load the page extracts all unique thematic keywords (material, motion, color, type, light, grid, etc.) into a Highlight Registry with CSS::highlight(keyword-amber) { background-color: #e8c5474d; color: #111 }. A scroll-driven wavefront (one rAF loop reads scrollY) highlights the keyword group whose centroid Y coordinate the wavefront has passed, so as the visitor reads down the page, words light up amber as the wave reaches them, creating a temporal ripple of annotation across the text. Clicking a keyword fires a named highlight that persists and propagates that keyword's occurrences across ALL paragraphs simultaneously. Distinct from anchor-annotated-index (CSS anchor-positioning tethers a panel into the margin on row hover; no text-range highlighting; no Custom Highlight API), manifesto-essay (long-read essay with margin footnotes and pull-quotes; no keyword synchronization; static text), and has-relational-reveal (CSS :has() relational chain dims/lights cards by data-tag; works on card DOM elements not text ranges). The first paragraph already has two keywords highlighted at scroll=0. @supports (CSS.highlights) gate; fallback is background-color on <mark> spans. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: identical. Guardrail: Highlight registry rebuilt on window resize; reduced-motion disables wavefront scrolling (all highlights applied on load). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.
